title: Exit Analysis: Timing, Multiples & IRR Sensitivity
learning_objectives: Compute IRR and MOIC under different exit timing and valuation scenarios
Build a multi-dimensional sensitivity table showing IRR across exit multiples and holding periods
Explain why IRR is highly sensitive to holding period even at the same MOIC
Compare IPO vs M&A exit paths using probability-weighted expected value
Identify the exit timing that maximizes IRR given a range of market multiple assumptions

blocks.fintech_hook: When Advent International sold TransUnion via IPO in 2015, their exit timing was driven by credit market conditions, comp valuations, and the IPO window being open after two years of strong returns. Exit analysis is as much about market timing as company performance.
blocks.explanation: Cover the three exit types (IPO, M&A, secondary), exit timing sensitivity on IRR (same 3x MOIC at year 3 = 44% IRR, at year 5 = 25% IRR, at year 7 = 17% IRR), exit multiples vs entry multiples, strategic vs financial buyer premiums (20-30%), lockup periods after IPO, EBITDA at exit as function of EBITDA growth rate and holding period, how debt paydown converts to equity value over time.

code_example.description: Entry: $400M EV, $140M equity (35%), debt $260M which pays down to $100M by year 5. EBITDA grows from $50M at entry. Exit at year 3, 5, and 7 under different EBITDA multiples. Compute equity value = exit EV - remaining debt. Compute MOIC = exit equity / entry equity. Compute IRR using scipy.optimize.brentq solving for r in sum(CF/(1+r)^t) = 0. Show 3x3 table: rows=holding period (3,5,7 years), cols=exit multiple (7x,9x,11x), values=IRR. Show that year-3 exit at 7x can beat year-7 exit at 9x.

blocks.scenario: Deciding whether to exit a portfolio cybersecurity company via IPO this quarter or wait 18 months for better metrics. Company has 300M revenue, 35% EBITDA margins ($105M EBITDA), 20% growth. Public comps trade at 12x EBITDA but may compress if rates rise. Advent invested $200M equity 4 years ago at entry EBITDA of $60M (10x entry). Debt has been paid from $800M to $400M.
constraints: Exit window is open for roughly 6-9 months before possible rate hike closes IPO market
LP distributions are overdue — the fund is in year 7 of a 10-year life
Management team has change-of-control bonuses triggered at exit worth $25M
Public market IPO requires 6-month lockup before Advent can sell remaining shares

stakeholder_pressures.pressure: Seeking liquidity after 7 years; concerned about fund vintage and need for distributions
Prefers waiting for higher valuation multiples; concerned about market timing risk
IPO window is narrowing; Fed policy and rate environment may compress multiples in 18 months

blocks.intro: Build the full exit analysis for Advent's cybersecurity portfolio company — IRR sensitivity table and probability-weighted expected value comparison.

steps.title: Build IRR Sensitivity Table
steps.description: Construct a 3x4 sensitivity table (3 holding periods: now/1yr, 18 months, and 24 months; 4 exit EBITDA multiples: 9x, 11x, 12x, 14x) for the Advent scenario. Show that with debt paid down and growing EBITDA, the IRR is highest for near-term exits despite lower multiples.

code_structure.setup: Define entry parameters: 200M equity, 800M initial debt, 60M entry EBITDA at year 0, 20% growth rate
code_structure.debt_schedule: Model debt paydown: 800M at entry, declining by ~100M per year to 600M at year 1, 500M at year 1.5, 400M at year 2
code_structure.exit_ev_calc: For each multiple and year: Exit EV = exit EBITDA * multiple
code_structure.equity_value: Equity value at exit = Exit EV - Remaining Debt
code_structure.moic_irr: MOIC = Exit Equity Value / Entry Equity (200M); IRR solved via scipy.optimize.brentq for the internal rate of return
code_structure.output: Matrix showing IRR (%) for each (holding_period, exit_multiple) pair

steps.business_implication: Demonstrates the power of debt paydown and EBITDA growth in early years. A near-term exit at 11x can yield higher IRR (38-42%) than a delayed exit at 12-14x due to compound effects of debt reduction and multiple expansion offset by time value. This justifies urgency.

steps.title: Probability-Weighted Expected Value Analysis
steps.description: Assign probabilities to 3 scenarios: (a) IPO now at 12x EBITDA with 105M exit EBITDA, (b) wait 18 months IPO at 14x EBITDA, (c) market downturn scenario: wait 18 months IPO at 9x EBITDA. Compute expected IRR for 'exit now' vs 'wait.' Calculate break-even probability for high-multiple scenario that makes waiting worthwhile.

code_structure.scenario_a: Exit now: 105M EBITDA * 12x = 1,260M EV; debt at 600M; equity = 660M; MOIC = 660/200 = 3.3x; IRR = immediate (or 0 holding period as benchmark)
code_structure.scenario_b: Wait 18mo favorable: 105M * 1.20^1.5 = 126M EBITDA * 14x = 1,764M EV; debt at 500M; equity = 1,264M; MOIC = 6.32x; IRR at 1.5yr
code_structure.scenario_c: Wait 18mo downside: 126M * 9x = 1,134M EV; debt at 500M; equity = 634M; MOIC = 3.17x; IRR at 1.5yr
code_structure.probabilities: Assign: P(now) = certain (1.0 if exit); P(14x in 18mo) = 0.40; P(9x in 18mo) = 0.60
code_structure.breakeven_calc: Solve for probability p such that: p * IRR(14x scenario) + (1-p) * IRR(9x scenario) = IRR(exit now)
code_structure.output: Expected IRR comparison and break-even probability

steps.business_implication: Shows that even with optimistic 40% probability of 14x multiple in 18 months, the expected IRR of waiting may not beat exiting now—especially when LP pressure and fund vintage are factored in. The break-even probability is likely above market consensus, making immediate exit the rational choice.
